Facts.text: Pelagius II begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Asturian queen Adosinda was put in the monastery of San Juan de Pravia, where she lived out the rest of her life, to prevent her kin from retaking the throne from Mauregatus.
French Huguenots and Roman Catholics sign a peace treaty
Duke of Savoy signs on to the League of Augsburg, opposing France
The first lion exhibited in America is displayed in Boston
French, Bavarian, and Saxon armies occupy Prague
France bans Jesuit enorde
Catholic Apostolic Prefecture of the United States established
First national Thanksgiving in America
Official opening of Thomas Telford's Pontcysyllte Aqueduct, carrying the Llangollen canal 126 feet above the River Dee. Longest aqueduct in the UK and highest canal aqueduct in the world.
Kappa Alpha fraternity is established at Union College in Schenectady, New York; thought to be the oldest continuously maintained collegiate society in US
First streetcar railway in America starts operating in New York City with a 12-cent fare
HMS Beagle leaves Tahiti for New Zealand
The University of Notre Dame is founded
Alfred de Mussets "Un Caprice" premieres in Paris
West Virginia created as a result of dispute over slavery with Virginia
-Dec 2] Mine Run campaign, VA
Skirmish at Sylvan Brutal/Waynesboro, Georgia
Battle of Papudo: The Spanish navy engages a combined Peruvian-Chilean fleet north of Valparaiso, Chile
Refrigerated railroad car patented by JB Sutherland of Detroit
King Lafia "Absalamu" of Nikki signs accord with France
Hawaiian Sugar Planters Association forms
1st large indoor football game, University of Chicago beats University of Michigan 7-6
Amos Alonzo Stagg of University of Chicago creates American football huddle
-27) Snow and ice storm over US kills 455
SS Portland “The Titanic of New England” leaves for Cape Cod, shipwrecked off Cape Ann, all 192 on board killed
Italy and Britain sign an agreement fixing the frontier between their colonies of Eritrea and Sudan in East Africa
Sigma Alpha Mu is founded at the City College of New York by eight young Jewish men
Phi Sigma Sigma is founded at Hunter College in New York City
Russian kingdom forbids Polish congregation of speakers
Battleship HMS Bulwark explodes at Sheerness Harbour, England, 788 die
Greece declares war on Germany
NHL forms with Montreal Canadiens, Montreal Maroons, Toronto Arenas, Ottawa Senators & Quebec Bulldogs; National Hockey Association disbands
The new government of Russia offers an armistice to Germany and Austria-Hungary
The Podgorica Assembly votes for "union of the people", declaring assimilation into the Kingdom of Serbia
Danish composer Rued Langgaard's "Music of the Spheres" (Sfærernes Musik) premieres at Konzerthaus in Karlsruhe, Germany; it is not performed in Denmark until 1969
First successful Technicolor movie "The Toll of the Sea" premieres at the Rialto Theatre in NYC
Mongolian People's Republic is proclaimed
Netherlands & Germany sign trade agreement
Philip Barry's play "Holiday" premieres in New York City
Camille Chautemps becomes French premier for the second time
Turkish parliament bestows President Mustafa Kemal with the surname Atatürk, meaning 'Father of the Turks' as part of the country's modernization effort
Four Soviet soldiers are killed on the Finnish-Russian border
Leidse students strike
Nazi Germany began walling off the Jewish Ghetto in Warsaw
British troops conquer Belhamed, Sidi Rezegh & El Duda
General Alan Cunningham relieved of command of British 8th Army in North Africa
Lebanon independence first proclaimed by France
Anti-fascist Council for National Liberation (AVNOJ) forms
First Allied Marines move into Antwerp Harbor
During snow storm, school bus crashes, kills 15 (Washington)
The Constituent Assembly of India formally adopts a new constitution
China enters Korean conflict, sends troops across Yalu River
1st 3D feature film "Bwana Devil" directed by Arch Oboler premieres in Los Angeles, advertised as "The Miracle of the Age!!! A LION in your lap! A LOVER in your arms!"
KBOI (now KBCI) TV channel 2 in Boise, ID (CBS) begins broadcasting
WJHL TV channel 11 in Johnson City, TN (CBS) begins broadcasting
Yamada Koun, leader of Sanbo Kyodan line of Zen, found 1st awakening
Emergency crisis proclaimed in Cyprus
American weightlifter Charles Vinci wins bantamweight gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics with world-record 3-lift (snatch, clean & jerk, overhead press) total of 342.5 kilograms
Egil Danielsen of Norway sets a new world record throw of 85.71m to win the men's javelin gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics
TV game show "The Price Is Right", hosted by Bill Cullen, debuts on NBC

USSR coxless pairs scull rower Viktor Ivanov wins Olympic silver medal; in his excitement he drops his medal, it sinks in Lake Wendouree (Victoria, Australia) and is later recovered by a local teenager
WCVB TV channel 5 in Boston, MA (ABC) begins broadcasting
Minneapolis-St Paul baseball club takes the name Twins
For 2nd time in his career, St Louis' Jerry Norton has 4 interceptions
Pro Baseball Rules Committee votes 8-1 against legalizing the spitball
Caribbean Air Transport Me NV (CLTM Airlines) forms
The Beatles record "Ask Me Why" at EMI Studio, London
29th Heisman Trophy Award: Roger Staubach, Navy (QB)
Explorer 18 spacecraft launched by NASA
Dodgers 2nd baseman Jim Lefebvre is voted NL Rookie of Year
1st major tidal power plant opens at Rance estuary, France
Browns' Carl Ward sets club record with a 104-yd kickoff return
Cloud burst over Lisbon kills about 450
Rock band Cream gives "final" concert at Royal Albert Hall, London, England - but re-unites briefly for a short tour in 2005
35th Heisman Trophy Award: Steve Owens, Oklahoma (RB)
In Basse-Terre, Guadeloupe, 1.5 inches (38.1 mm) of rain falls in a single minute, the heaviest recorded rainfall in history
Bomb explosion at the Film Centre Cinema, in O’Connell Bridge House in Dublin, Ireland
NFL NY Giant placekicker Péte Gogolák scores team record 8 PAT (points after touchdown) in 62-10 win over Philadelphia Eagles at Yankee Stadium, NYC
Nixon's personal secretary Rose Mary Woods, tells a federal court she accidentally caused part of 18½ minute gap in a key Watergate tape
Approximately 140 die when suspension bridge collapses in Nepal
Greenidge scores 107 in 2nd innings of Test Cricket debut v India
France performs nuclear test at Fangataufa Island
US Federal jury finds Lynette Fromme guilty of attempted assassination
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
East Germany strips singer-songwriter and poet Wolf Biermann of his citizenship while he tours West Germany
'Vrillon', claiming to be the representative of the 'Ashtar Galactic Command', takes over Britain's Southern Television for six minutes at 5:12 PM.
10 die as fire erupts at Holiday Inn in Rochester, NY
1st lesbian themed TV movie - "A Question of Love"
Christina Crawford autobiography "Mommie Dearest" reaches best-seller status
Intl Olympic Committee votes to readmit China after 21 years
Twins John Castino & Jays Alfredo Griffin tie for AL Rookie of Year
Columbia mated to SRBs & external tank at Vehicle Assembly Building
Clyde King named New York Yankee manager
£26 million worth of gold, diamonds, and cash is stolen from the Brink's-Mat warehouse at Heathrow Airport, London, in one of the world's greatest robberies
John W Mercom Jr announces New Orleans Saints are up for sale for $75 million
US & Iraq re-establish diplomatic relations
23rd NASA Space Shuttle Mission (61-B): Atlantis 2 launches
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Random House buys former US PresidentRichard Nixon's memoirs for $3,000,000
Trial begins in Israel for John Demjanjuk, accused of being Nazi prison guard "Ivan the Terrible" at Treblinka concentration camp
Alexander Volkov, Sergei Krikalev & Jean-Loup Chretien launch
Pioneer 6's closest approach to Earth since 1965 launch (1.87 M km)
Comores coup under Bob Benard
Luis Alberto Lacalle wins Uruguayan presidential election
Rafael Callejas installed as president of Honduras
Buffalo Bills become the sixth first-place NFL team to lose on the same weekend
Matsushita purchases MCA for $6.6 billion
Premier Mazowiecki of Poland resigns
US proposes addition to UN resolution that would require Iraq's withdrawal from Kuwait by January 1
Condoms are handed out to thousands of NY High School students
British geneticist Alec Jeffreys who developed widely-used techniques for DNA fingerprinting, becomes honorary freeman of the city of Leicester
New Zealand scores 348 for 8 in 49 overs in an ODI against India in Nagpur
Baseball owners approve interleague play, 26-4
Wash Capitals final game at USAir Arena, retire Rod Langway's #5
India accepts Pakistan's offer of a ceasefire in Kashmir
Supersonic airplane the Concorde makes its last ever flight, returning to Bristol, England.
Ruzhou School massacre: Yan Yanming stabs and kills eight boys and seriously wounds another four in a school dormitory in Ruzhou, China
The last international rugby match before demolition of Dublin's famous Lansdowne Road Stadium is Ireland's 61-17 win over the Pacific Islanders
Ten coordinated attacks by Pakistan-based terrorists kill 164 and injure more than 250 people in Mumbai, India
NATO forces in Afghanistan attack a Pakistani checkpoint in a friendly fire incident, killing 24 soldiers and wounding 13 others
10 children are killed and 15 people are injured after a Syrian government Jet drops a cluster bomb on a playground
The cost of Hurricane Sandy to New York is announced to be $32 Billion
UK nonprofit Raspberry Pi releases its $5 Zero computer, sells out in a day
Factory explosion in Chinese port city Ningbo kills at least 2, injuries 30, flattens buildings
Presidential elections held in Honduras with allegations of electoral fraud and resulting violent protests
Australian surfer Stephanie Gilmore is handed her record equalling 7th world title as American contender Lakey Peterson is a shock 2nd round elimination at the season-ending Maui Pro in Hawaii
Chinese scientist He Jiankui claims to have made the first genetically edited baby
First multidrug randomized control trial to treat Ebola started in Democratic Republic of Congo announced by the WHO
General Motors announces it will close five factories in North America, cutting 14,000 jobs
NASA's InSight mission lands on Mars after a 7 month voyage
Third of a species begin dying (23,000) over a two day period, the spectacled flying foxes, unable to survive heatwave of 42 degrees in northern Queensland, Australia
Ukraine declares martial law in areas bordering Russia after Russia seizes three of its naval vessels
6.4 magnitude earthquake centered on Durres strikes Albania, with at least 51 killed and hundreds injured
Turkey gives life sentences to 337 military officers and others involved in 2016 coup
WHO labels COVID-19 variant Omicron a "variant of concern", with its dozens of new mutations, after it emerges in Botswana and South Africa
2,500 people pose naked for photographer Spencer Tunick on Australia's Bondi Beach to raise awareness of skin cancer
Large mudslide after intense rain tears across Italian island of Ischia, killing at least eight people, on an island where 50% of the houses were illegally built
Rare public protests held in various Chinese cities over the governments restrictive COVID-19 policies, after 10 people die in an apartment fire in Urumqi, Xinjiang
Booker literary Prize won by Irish writer Paul Lynch for his novel "Prophet Song"
Sierra Leone declares nationwide curfew after attempted coup when gunmen tried to break into country's largest military barracks in Freetown
Two-day extension to Gaza ceasefire announced by mediating country Qatar, with a fourth set of hostages exchanged by both sides
Second Triumvirate alliance of Roman leaders formed byOctavian(later CaesarAugustus),Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, andMark Antony
Vlad the Impalerdefeats Basarab Laiota with the help of Stephen the Great and Stephen V Báthory and becomes the ruler of Wallachia for the third time
PopeInnocent Xcondemns Peace of Westphalia; the treaty that ended the Thirty Years' War recognising Dutch Republic's Independence and Catholic and Lutherans as equal
French KingLouis XIVdeclares war on Netherlands
KingJames IIescapes back to London
British explorer CaptainJames Cookis the first European to visit Maui in the Sandwich Islands (now Hawaii)
Last weekly installment ofCharles Dickens' "A Tale of Two Cities" is published in literary periodical "All the Year Round"
"Alice in Wonderland" byLewis Carrollis published in America
Addressing the Chamber of Commerce in Cincinnati, US PresidentWoodrow Wilsondeclares that 'The business of neutrality is over. The nature of modern war leaves no state untouched'
Amateur American tennis starsBobby Riggsand Frank Kovacs turn professional after being suspended by the US Lawn Tennis Association for undisclosed financial gain
"Casablanca," directed by Michael Curtiz and starringHumphrey BogartandIngrid Bergman, premieres at Hollywood Theater, NYC (Academy Award for Best Picture 1943)
MGM releases last of 9Judy Garland-Mickey Rooneymovies - "Girl Crazy", a film adaptation ofGeorge GershwinandIra Gershwin's stage musical
Heinrich Himmlerorders the destruction of theAuschwitzand Birkenau crematoria
"Brief Encounter", based onNoël Coward's one-act play "Still Life", directed byDavid Leanand starring Celia Johnson andTrevor Howardis released
Charlie "Bird" Parkerleads a recording session with Dizzy Gillespie andMiles Davisfor the Savoy label, marketed as the "greatest jazz session ever"
1stPolaroidcamera sold for $89.75 in Boston at the Jordan Marsh department store. The Land Camera model 95 becomes prototype for all Polaroid Land cameras for next 15 years
Test Cricket debut ofColin Cowdreyv Australia at Gabba
Australian sprinterBetty Cuthbertwins the coveted 100m gold medal in 11.5 at the Melbourne Olympics; takes sprint double winning 200m final four days later
The Beatles re-record "Please Please Me" at a faster tempo, based on producerGeorge Martin's suggestion
Cincinnati Reds 2nd basemanPete Rosewins NL Rookie of Year
France launches 1st satellite, 92 lb (42 kg) A1-capsule (Astérix), named after the popular French comic character
34th Heisman Trophy Award:O.J. Simpson, Southern Cal (RB)
Lottery for Selective Service draftees bill signed by US PresidentRichard Nixon
Boston Red Sox center fielderFred Lynnbecomes the first rookie ever to be named American League MVP
British punk rockersSex Pistolsrelease their debut single "Anarchy In The UK"
Former West German ChancellorWilly Brandtis elected chairman of the Socialist International (1976-92)
Ringo Starrreleases single "Hey Baby"
Mike Schmidtis unanimous choice as NL MVP
Howard Cossell calls his last fight after being disgusted byLarry Holmes-Tex Cobb mismatch
After 518 goals & 14 years with Montreal Canadiens,Guy LaFleurretires abruptly
India's Congress Party led by then Prime MinisterRajiv Gandhiloses the general election to an opposition front led by V. P. Singh
Mikhail Gorbachevtells Iraq to get out of Kuwait
Dolphins QBDan Marinosets NFL record with 343rd touchdown pass
Tony Blairis the first British Prime Minister to address the parliament of the Republic of Ireland
Steve Yzermanscores his 600th career goal
"Like You'll Never See Me Again" single released byAlicia Keys(NAACP Image Awards Outstanding Music Video, Outstanding Song 2008, Billboard Song of the Year 2008)
Christopher HitchensandTony Blairdebate - is religion a force for good in the world, in a Monk Debate, Toronto, Canada
Due to a street tirade captured on video,Alec Baldwin's show "Up Late with Alec Baldwin" is cancelled after only five episodes

British Mercedes driverLewis Hamiltonfinishes 2nd in season-ending Abu Dhabi Grand Prix at Yas Marina Circuit to claim his 4th F1 World Drivers Championship; wins title by 46 points fromSebastian Vettel
DC superhero film "Aquaman" directed by James Wan starringJason MomoaandAmber Heardpremieres in London
Dutch driverMax Verstappenconfirms his third consecutive world F1 drivers championship with his record 19th GP win of the season in Abu Dhabi; his Red Bull team retains constructors title
Elon Muskvisits Israel after Starlink satellite agreement, amid controversy after he liked an antisemitic post on X, prompting widespread criticism and loss of advertisers on X

First US cabinet meeting, held atGeorge Washington's home in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Secretary of State Thomas Jefferson, Secretary of the Treasury Alexander Hamilton, Secretary of War Henry Knox, and Attorney General Edmund Randolph attend.
Republican calendar replaces Gregorian calendar in France
English archaeologistHoward Carteropens Tutankhamun's nearly intact tomb in Egypt
Japanese naval carrier force left its base & moves east toward Pearl Harbor
Go-Daigo, 96th Emperor of Japan (1318-39) who successfully overthrew the Kamakura shogunate, establishing the Kenmu Restoration (1333-36), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1339)
Princess Catherine of Portugal [Infanta Catarina], Portuguese princess, nun, writer, and daughter of King Edward of Portugal and his wife Eleanor of Aragon, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1463)
John Harvard, English clergyman and benefactor of Harvard College, born in Southwark, Surrey, England (d. 1638)
Henry Dunster, American clergyman and first President of Harvard College, born in Bolholt, Bury, Lancashire (d. 1659)
Carl Rosier, Dutch baroque composer, born in Prince-Bishopric of Liège, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1725)
Andreas Anton Schmelzer, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1701)
William Derham, English clergyman, natural theologian and scientist, born in Stoulton, Worcestershire (d. 1735)
Pedro de Peralta y Barnuevo, Peruvian poet (Obras Dramaticas), born in Lima, Peru (d. 1743)
Jean Jacques d'Ortous de Mairan, French geophysicist, born in Béziers, France (d. 1771)
Theophilus Cibber, English actor and writer, born in London, England (d. 1758)
Artemas Ward, American politician and soldier (major general during the American Revolutionary War), born in Shrewsbury, Massachusetts (d. 1800)
William Cowper, English pre-romantic poet (His Task), born in Berkhamsted, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1800)
Charles-Joseph Panckoucke, French publisher (Mercure de France), born in Lille, France (d. 1798)
Karl Siegmund von Seckendorff, German composer, born in Erlangen, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1785)
Georg Forster, German writer, naturalist (A Voyage Round the World) and revolutionary, born in Nassenhuben (Mokry Dwór), Royal Prussia, Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (d. 1794)
Carl Philipp Fohr, German painter and cartoonist, born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1818)
Cornelis Pieter Jacob Elout, Dutch military Major-General who served in Sumatra (2nd Padri War, 1831-37), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1843)
Marià Obiols, Catalan composer, conductor, music director (Gran Teatre del Liceu, 1847-88), and music educator, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1888)
William Armstrong, 1st Baron Armstrong and British industralist and inventor (hydraulic crane Armstrong Gun), born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1900)
Zeng Guofan, Chinese statesman, military general and Confucian scholar of the late Qing dynasty, born in Xiangxiang, Hunan Province, Qing Empire (d. 1872)
William H. T. Walker, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 1864)
Louis Lacombe, French pianist and composer, born in Bourges, France (d. 1884)
Thomas Tellefsen, Norwegian pianist and composer, born in Trondheim, Norway (d. 1874)
Alfred Moore Scales, American politician (45th Governor of North Carolina), born in Reidsville, North Carolina (d. 1892)
René Goblet, French politician (41st Prime Minister of France. 1886-87), born in Aire-sur-la-Lys, France (d. 1905)
Mary Edwards Walker, American surgeon and women's rights leader and only woman to receive Medal of Honor (bravery during Civil War), born in Oswego, New York (d. 1919)
Rudolph Koenig, German physicist, born in Königsberg, Prussia (d. 1901)
Maria Feodorovna, Princess of Denmark and Empress of Russia, born in Yellow Palace, Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1928)
Henricus van de Wetering, Archbishop of Utrecht (1895-1929), born in Hoogland, Netherlands (d. 1929)
Yamamoto Gonnohyōe, Japanese admiral and politician (Prime Minister of Japan 1913-1914, 1923-1924), born in Kagoshima, Japan (d. 1933)
Bat Masterson, American gunfighter in the Wild West, born in Henriville, Canada East (d. 1921)
Ferdinand de Saussure, Swiss linguist (Mémoire sur le système primitif des voyelles dans les langues indo-européennes), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1913)
Katharine Drexel, American philanthropist and Roman Catholic Saint, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1955)
Marc Aurel Stein, Hungarian-British archaeologist, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1943)
Herman Gorter, Dutch socialist and poet (May, Tiny Hero's Poem), born in Wormerveer, Netherlands (d. 1927)
Earl Ross Drake, American violinist and composer (The Mite and the Mighty), born in Aurora, Illinois (d. 1916)
Hugh Duffy, American Baseball HOF outfielder (Triple Crown & MLB record .440 batting average, single season 1894 Boston Beaneaters), born in Cranston, Rhode Island (d. 1954)
Maud of Wales, Queen of Norway, spouse of King Haakon VII, born in Marlborough House, London (d. 1938)
Fred Herd, Scottish golfer (US Open 1898), born in St. Andrews, Scotland (d. 1954)
Edmond Fleg[enheimer], Swiss-French author (Ecoute Israel), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1963)
Bart van der Leck, Dutch painter, and co-founder of the De Stijl movement, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1958)
(Marshall) "Major" Taylor, American track cyclist (first African-American world champion; 1899 World Track C'ships), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1932)
Heinrich Brüning, German politician and Chancellor of Germany (1930-32), born in Münster, Province of Westphalia, German Empire (d. 1970)
Ernest de Silva, Sri Lankan business magnate and philanthropist, born in Colombo, British Ceylon (d. 1957)
Franz Jung, German writer and political activist, born in Nysa (d. 1963)
Albert Dieudonné, French actor and novelist (Backbiters, Napoleon), born in Paris, France (d. 1976)
Joe Guyon, American NFL halfback (Canton, Cleveland, Oorang, Rock I, etc), born in White Earth, Minnesota (d. 1971)
James Charles McGuigan, Catholic cardinal archbishop of Toronto, born in Hunter River, Prince Edward Island, Canada (d. 1974)
Bertil Lindblad, Swedish astronomer (Milky Way system), born in Örebro, Sweden (d. 1965)
Bill Wilson, American co-founder (alcoholics anonymous), born in East Dorset, Vermont (d. 1971)
Bruno Hauptmann, German kidnapper of Charles Augustus Lindbergh III, born in Kamenz, Saxony, Germany (d. 1936)
Maurice Rose, American army officer (WWi; WWII), highest-ranking American killed by enemy fire in Europe, born in Middletown, Connecticut (d. 1945)
Philip Idenburg, Dutch statistician, born in Hillegersberg, Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1995)
Alberto Morin, Puerto Rican actor (Two Mules for Sister Sara, Rio Grande), born in San Juan, Puerto Rico (d. 1989)
Gerrit Jan van der Veen [Wolffensperger], Dutch resistance fighter, born in Amsterdam (d. 1944)
Maurice "Mac" McDonald, American fast food pioneer, co-founder of McDonald's, born in Manchester, New Hampshire (d. 1971)
Armand Frappier, French Canadian physician and microbiologist, born in Salaberry-de-Valleyfield, Quebec, Canada (d. 1991)
Bob Johnson, American baseball outfielder (8 x MLB All Star; Philadelphia Athletics, Boston Red Sox), born in Pryor, Oklahoma (d. 1982)
Emlyn Williams, Welsh actor (Ivanhoe, I Accuse!), and playwright (The Corn Is Green), born in Mostyn, Wales (d. 1987)
Sandro Fuga, Italian composer and pianist, born in Mogliano, Veneto, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1994)
"Kansas City" Frank [Melrose], American jazz and blues pianist, born in Sumner, Illinois (d. 1941)
Henry "Hot Lips" Levine, British-American dixieland jazz trumpeter and bandleader, born in London England (d. 1989)
Ruth Patrick, American botanist and limnologist, born in Topeka, Kansas (d. 2013)
Charles Forte, Italian-British hotel magnate (Savoy), born in Mortale, Italy (d. 2007)
Philipp Mohler, German composer, born in Kaiserslautern, Germany (d. 1982)
Frances Dee, American actress (Of Human Bondage), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2004)
Cyril Cusack, South African actor (The Day of the Jackal; Fahrenheit 451), born in Durban, South Africa (d. 1993)
Raymond Scheyven, Belgian banker, minister of Economic Affairs, and founder of the Socrates Service - a WWII underground civil resistance group, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1987)
Samuel Reshevsky, Polish-American chess grandmaster (World C'ship 3rd 1948; 8 x US C'ship gold), born in Ozorków, Poland (d. 1992)
Eric Sevareid, American author and News correspondent (CBS), born in Velva, North Dakota (d. 1992)
Gunnar Sønstevold, Norwegian jazz pianist, and orchestral and film score composer (Litany In Atlanta; The Dorian Cage), born in Elverum, Norway (d. 1991)
Foy Draper, American athlete (Olympic gold 4 x 100m relay 1936 WR 39.8s), born in Georgetown, Texas (d. 1943)
Earl Wild, American composer and pianist (Caesar's Hour, 1952-56; NBC Symphony, 1937-44), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2010)
Herbert Joeks [Herman Hugten], Dutch actor (Snip & Snap, Pipo de clown), born in Haarlem, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Bob Elliott, MLB player (Pittsburgh Pirates, Boston Braves), born in San Francisco (d. 1966)
Mareo Ishiketa, Japanese composer, born in Wakayama, Japan (d. 1996)
Adele Jergens, American actress (Dark Past, Fuller Brush Man), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2002)
Nesuhi Ertegün, Turkish–American jazz record producer and label executive (Atlantic Records; Warner Music), born in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire (now Turkey) (d. 1989)
Patricio Aylwin, Chilean lawyer, politician and President of Chile (1990-94), born in Viña del Mar, Chile (d. 2016)
Frederik Pohl, American sci-fi author (3 Hugo, Gateway, Bipohl), born in New York City (d. 2013)
Daniel Petrie, Canadian television and movie director, born in Glace Bay, Nova Scotia, Canada (d. 2004)
István Sárközy, Hungarian composer, born in Pesterzsébet, Budapest, Hungary (d. 2002)
Paul Rodenko, Dutch poet and author (Stolen Lover), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Françoise Gilot, French painter, designer, and author (Life with Picasso), born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France (d. 2023)
Verghese Kurien, Indian engineer (billion-litre idea), born in Calicut, India (d. 2012)
Étienne Gailly, Belgian long distance runner (famous Olympic bronze finish 1948), born in Beringen, Belgium (d. 1971)
Patricia "Pat" Phoenix, British actress (Coronation Street, L Shaped Room), born in Fallowfield, Manchester, England (d. 1986)
Bobby Sharp, American piano player, songwriter (“Unchain My Heart”), and drug rehab counselor, born in Topeka, Kansas (d. 2013)
George Segal, American painter and sculptor of lifelike mixed-media figures (Bus Driver), born in New York City (d. 2000)
Irwin Hoffman, American conductor (Vancouver Symphony, 1952-64; Chicago Symphony, 1964-70; Costa Rica National Symphony, 1987-2001), born in New York City (d. 2018)
Jasu Patel, Indian cricket spin bowler (7 Tests, 29 wickets, BB 9-69 v Aust 1959; Gujarat), born in Ahmedabad, India (d. 1992)
Michael Holliday [Norman Milne], British pop singer ("The Story of My Life"), born in Liverpool, England (d. 1963)
Victor Grinich, Croatian-American semiconductor industry pioneer and member of the "traitorous eight" (Fairchild Semiconductor), born in Aberdeen, Washington (d. 2000)
Eugene Istomin, American concert pianist (Leventritt Award, 1943; Istomin-Stern-Rose Trio), born in New York City (d. 2003)
Mauro Bortolotti, Italian composer, born in Narni, Italy (d. 2007)
Michael Butler, American theatrical producer (Hair; Lenny), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2022)
Ernie Coombs, American-Canadian children's entertainer (Mr. Dressup), born in Lewiston, Maine (d. 2001)
John Carter, American actor (Scarface, The Hoax, Badlands, Max-Falcon Crest), born in Center Ridge, Arkansas (d. 2015)
Don Tregonning, Australian tennis player and coach, born in Melbourne, Victoria
John Barton, British theatre director and co-founder of the Royal Shakespeare Company, born in London, England (d. 2018)
Betta St John [Betty Jean Striegler], American-English stage and screen actress, singer and dancer (Dream Wife; Carousel; Corridors of Blood), born in Hawthorne, California (d. 2023)
Joyce McCartan, Irish peace campaigner, born in Banbridge, County Down, Ireland (d. 1995)
Lorraine Macleod, American dancer (Girls Just Want to Have Fun), born in Maine (d. 1995)

Slavko Avsenik, Slovenian polka and Oberkrain ethnic accordionist, piano player, and composer (Avsenik Brothers Ensemble), born in Begunje na Gorenjskem, Kingdom of Yugoslavia (now Slovenia) (d. 2015)
Berthold Leibinger, German entrepreneur (Trumpf) and philanthropist, born in Stuttgart, Baden-Württemberg, Germany (d. 2018)
Adolfo Pérez Esquivel, Argentine painter and activist (1980 Nobel Peace Prize), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina
Adrianus Johannes Simonis, Dutch clergyman, Catholic cardinal and Archbishop of Utrecht (1983-2007), born in Lisse, Netherlands (d. 2020)
Giuliana Chenal-Minuzzo, Italian downhill skier (Olympic bronze 1952, 60), born in Vallonara di Marostica, Italy
Alan Stout, American composer, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2018)
Lyudmila Shevtsova, Russian athlete (Olympic gold USSR 800m 1960), born in Taman, Krasnodar Krai, Russia
Marian Mercer, American actress and singer (Dean Martin Show), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 2011)
Jan Liberda, Polish soccer forward (35 caps; Polonia Bytom), born in Bytom, Poland (d. 2020)
Lalith Athulathmudali, Sri Lankan minister (United National Party), born in Colombo, Ceylon
Margaret Boden, British philosopher (Research Professor of Cognitive Science in the Department of Informatics at the University of Sussex), born in London (d. 2025)
Bob Babbitt [Robert Kreinar], Hungarian-American session bassist (Motown's The Funk Brothers), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2012)
Boris Yegorov, Soviet physician and cosmonaut (Voskhod I), born in Moscow, Soviet Union (d. 1994)
Porter Goss, American politician (Rep-R-Florida) and 1st Director of the Central Intelligence Agency, born in Waterbury, Connecticut
